steps.title: Spot the opening dash and what it marks
steps.content: An opening dash after "temperature" marks an appositive phrase, "around 225 degrees Fahrenheit." After this phrase comes the independent clause, "flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke."

steps.title: Apply the matching dashes rule
steps.content:
Structure: the sentence opens with "When heated to a particular temperature—around 225 degrees [blank] flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke." An opening dash appears after "temperature," so "around 225 degrees Fahrenheit" is a parenthetical inserted into the main clause. A pair of dashes requires a matching closing dash.

Rule: when a parenthetical is opened with a dash, it must be closed with a dash—not any other punctuation.

A. Fahrenheit — no closing dash. The parenthetical remains open, leaving "flaxseed oil will begin..." structurally attached to the parenthetical rather than the main clause. Eliminated.

B. Fahrenheit— — the closing dash matches the opening dash and seals off the parenthetical. "flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke" then correctly continues as the main clause. Correct.

C. Fahrenheit: — a colon inside a parenthetical enclosed by dashes is wrong; the closing mark must be another dash, not a colon. Eliminated.

D. Fahrenheit) — a closing parenthesis does not match an opening dash. Mixed brackets are not standard English. Eliminated.

B is correct.
